coredata.dc:title: Anchoring Bi4O5I2 and CDs on brown TiO2−x: S-scheme heterojunction mechanism for impressive degradation of several antibiotics under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Salmanzadeh-Jamadi, Zahra
Habibi-Yangjeh, Aziz
Xu, Cailing
Wang, Chundong
coredata.dc:description: Fully aware of the global water pollution caused by more consumption of antibiotics, we successfully prepared novel brown TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/carbon dots (B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs) photocatalysts through a facile strategy. The as-prepared samples were specified via XRD, FESEM, UV–vis DRS, XPS, EDS, TEM, HRTEM, FTIR, BET, BJH, EIS, photocurrent, and PL analyses. The ternary photocatalysts were utilized for the degradation of several antibiotics, including cephalexin (CEX), metronidazole (MNZ), and tetracycline (TC), under visible-light exposure provided by a 50 W LED. The B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite presented impressive activity in the photodegradation of CEX, MNZ, and TC antibiotics, which was 14.4, 39.8, and 49.7-folds of TiO2, and 4.71, 11.1, and 12.3 times as high as B-TiO2−x, respectively. The enhanced photo-degradation proficiency of optimal nanocomposite was assigned to the higher visible-light absorbance, reduced recombination of photo-induced carriers, and higher redox capability owing to the construction of S-scheme heterojunction amongst the components. In particular, ∙ O2 − and h+ showed a major role in the degradation of TC antibiotic. Moreover, the TC degradation mechanism was studied using LC-MS analysis. Finally, the optimized B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite displayed significant stability after four cycles, and it was displayed that the resultant solution after the degradation process has considerable biocompatibility through the growth of wheat seeds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Brown TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs
Brown TiO2−x
Antibiotic photodegradation
S-scheme mechanism
Visible-light photocatalyst

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD, (b) FT-IR, (c) Optical absorbance, (d) color, and (e) Plots of (αhν)2 versus hν for the photocatalysts.
(a) SEM, (b) TEM, (c) HRTEM, (d) EDX spectrum, and (e) EDX mapping of B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite.
XPS spectra of B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) photocatalyst.
(a) N2 gas sorption data, (b) BJH pore size plots, (c) PL spectra, (d) EIS, and (e) Transient photocurrents of the photocatalysts. (f) Mott–Schottky plot for Bi4O5I2 sample.

(a) Photodegradation and (b) degradation rate constant of TC over the photocatalysts. (C) Degradation rate constants of CEX, MNZ, and TC over the selected materials, (d) Impact of trapping agents during the photocatalytic reaction on B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite, and (e) The PL spectra for OHº trapping in the presence of B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite during various light irradiation times.
(a-c) S-scheme heterojunction and (d) type-II heterojunction mechanism for the B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs nanocomposites.
The possible pathway of TC degradation over B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) photocatalyst.
Growth of wheat seeds in (a, b, c) tap water, (d, e, f) TC-containing solution, and (g, h, i) photocatalytic-treated solution.
(a) The recycling runs in degradation of TC over B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) photocatalyst, (b) XRD pattern, and (c) FESEM image of the photocatalyst after photocatalysis.
Schematic plot for synthesis of B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposites.
Textural properties of TiO2−X, Bi4O5I2, TiO2−X/Bi4O5I2-2, and TiO2−X/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Anchoring Bi4O5I2 and CDs on brown TiO2−x: S-scheme heterojunction mechanism for impressive degradation of several antibiotics under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Laboratory of Special Function Materials and Structure Design of the Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Laboratory of Special Function Materials and Structure Design of the Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Laboratory of Special Function Materials and Structure Design of the Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Integrated Circuits, Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Integrated Circuits, Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Optical and Electronic Information, Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics, Optics Valley Laboratory, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, 430074, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fully aware of the global water pollution caused by more consumption of antibiotics, we successfully prepared novel brown TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/carbon dots (B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs) photocatalysts through a facile strategy. The as-prepared samples were specified via XRD, FESEM, UV–vis DRS, XPS, EDS, TEM, HRTEM, FTIR, BET, BJH, EIS, photocurrent, and PL analyses. The ternary photocatalysts were utilized for the degradation of several antibiotics, including cephalexin (CEX), metronidazole (MNZ), and tetracycline (TC), under visible-light exposure provided by a 50 W LED. The B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite presented impressive activity in the photodegradation of CEX, MNZ, and TC antibiotics, which was 14.4, 39.8, and 49.7-folds of TiO2, and 4.71, 11.1, and 12.3 times as high as B-TiO2−x, respectively. The enhanced photo-degradation proficiency of optimal nanocomposite was assigned to the higher visible-light absorbance, reduced recombination of photo-induced carriers, and higher redox capability owing to the construction of S-scheme heterojunction amongst the components. In particular, ∙ O2 − and h+ showed a major role in the degradation of TC antibiotic. Moreover, the TC degradation mechanism was studied using LC-MS analysis. Finally, the optimized B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite displayed significant stability after four cycles, and it was displayed that the resultant solution after the degradation process has considerable biocompatibility through the growth of wheat seeds.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, the growing global energy needs and concerns, and water contamination have encouraged researchers to explore about clean energy production and develop affordable technology for environmental detoxification [1–3]. The rapid development of modern industry and medical fields, not only causes global economic expansion and improves quality of life in societies, but also profoundly affects the ecological environment of our life through discharging refractory antibiotics in natural waters. These antibiotics are widely applied for disease prevention and the therapy of human and animal bacterial infections, and also agricultural activities [4]. However, due to the widespread utilization of antibiotics and their incomplete metabolism, a large amount of antibiotics is released and accumulated in the environment. Consequently, this is a severe threat to the health of living organisms, because the accumulation of these compounds leads to bacterial resistance and genetic mutation [5]. Among several technologies to tackle the mentioned crises, heterogeneous photocatalysis has recently received tremendous research attention for degrading recalcitrant and biologically resistant antibiotics using semiconductor-based photocatalysis, thanks to its green features [6–8]. Despite many appealing characteristics of TiO2, as one of the most famous semiconductors used in photocatalytic processes, it has some severe drawbacks, including insufficient harvesting of visible light, poor surface area, and quick recombination of produced carriers [9]. To overcome the bottlenecks of this photocatalyst, introducing oxygen vacancies (OVs) and Ti3+ self-doping within TiO2 (denoted as TiO2−x) have attracted a large number of research activities [10–12]. It is noteworthy that OVs and Ti3+ generations introduce defect states below to the conduction band bottom, resulting in more visible-light harvesting capability, improving electron transfer property, and providing more surface active sites for photocatalytic reactions [11,12]. Pursuant to the fabrication conditions and the electronic features of the generated defects, the color of titanium dioxide changes from white to colored samples, which causes extraordinary visible-light absorption. Nevertheless, the quick recombination of electron/hole (e−/h+) pairs has negatively affected on its practical applications [13]. This weakness can be covered by combining TiO2−x with semiconductors having suitable band energies and energy gaps.

In recent years, Bi-based semiconductors are frequently utilized in photocatalytic processes, thanks to their several appealing properties [14,15]. Among these semiconductors, non-stoichiometric BiOCl, BiOBr, and BiOI such as Bi12O17Cl2, Bi4O5Br2, Bi24O31Br10, Bi5O7Br, Bi3O4Br, and Bi5O7I have displayed impressive photocatalytic performances in comparison to the corresponding stoichiometric ones [16–21]. Moreover, carbon dots (CDs) are known as a fascinating class of carbon-containing materials with advantages of high stability, nontoxicity, and considerable visible-light absorbance, owing to their up-conversion feature. These materials not only can broaden optical absorbance range of photocatalysts, but also serve as an electron donor/acceptor species to promote e−/h+ separations [22–25].

In these regards, we anchored n-type Bi4O5I2 and CDs on the surface of brown TiO2−x (B-TiO2−x) using a facile strategy. The resultant ternary photocatalysts were characterized by XRD, FESEM, UV–vis DRS, EDS, TEM, HRTEM, XPS, FTIR, BET, BJH, EIS, photocurrent, and PL analyses. Among the photocatalysts, the B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite presented impressive activity in degradations of three antibiotics, including cephalexin (CEX), metronidazole (MNZ), and tetracycline (TC) at 25 °C under visible-light exposure. The impressively promoted activity of the ternary photocatalyst was attributed to significant visible-light absorption, efficiently segregating photo-generated charges, and more exposed surface area. The proposed S-scheme mechanism after n-n heterojunction construction between n-type B-TiO2−x and Bi4O5I2 could play a key role in the efficiently segregation of the photo-induced charges and promote photocatalytic performance. Moreover, the TC degradation mechanism was studied using LC-Ms analysis. Finally, it was displayed that the resultant solution after the degradation process has considerable biocompatibility through the growth of wheat seeds.

A Cecile 9000 spectrophotometer was utilized for recording the UV–Vis absorption spectra. The phase structure of the synthesized samples was conducted by a Philips Xpert using Cu Kα radiation. The XPS analysis was carried out via a ThermoFisher X-ray spectrometer. Moreover, the textural data were provided by N2 sorption isotherms using a Belsorp mini II instrument. The FTIR data were provided by a Perkin Elmer Spectrum RX I. The PL data were recorded using a PerkinElmer (LS 55) fluorescence spectrophotometer. For this aim, 0.005 g of the photocatalyst was added to 10 mL of water, and the excitation wavelength was 200 nm.The morphologies were observed by FESEM (Hitachi, S4800) and TEM (JEOL, JEM-2100 F) instruments. The electrochemical studies were conducted on a μAutolabIII/FRA2. The LC-Ms data were provided by a 2795 Waters HPLC separation system supplied with a C 18 column (Agilent).

The CDs and B-TiO2−x were synthesized by the procedures described elsewhere [26,27]. The binary B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2 nanocomposite with 10%, 20%, and 30% weight percentages of Bi4O5I2, were fabricated by hydrothermal method, and the resulting photocatalysts were labeled as B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2-1, B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2-2, and B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2-3, respectively. To synthesize B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2-2 nanocomposite, 0.4 g B-TiO2−x was dispersed into 120 mL water and sonicated for 10 min to get a B-TiO2−x suspension. Then, 0.165 g Bi (NO3)3·5 H2O was dispersed in 35 mL ethylene glycol, and it was added to the suspension, and strenuously stirred for one hour to yield a homogeneous system. Then, 0.03 g NaI was added to 10 mL water and stirred for 15 min. The resultant solution was added to the B-TiO2−x suspension and its solution pH was adjusted at 9 by using concentrated NaOH, and subsequently transferred into an autoclave and heated at 150 °C for 12 h. Finally, the obtained suspension was washed repeatedly with water and ethanol and dried.

For the synthesis of ternary B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite, where 1 mL is amount of the applied CDs, 0.4 g B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2-2 nanocomposite and 1 mL of CDs solution were ultrasound in 100 mL water for 15 min. Finally, the resultant precipitate was dried at 60 °C. The contents of anchored CDs over the B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2-2 nanocomposite were changed and the resultant photocatalysts were labeled as B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (0.5 mL), B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL), and B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1.5 mL).

The photodegradation of antibiotics was performed under visible-light exposure provided by a 50 W LED lamp with a broad emission in the range of 450–650 nm. Typically, 0.1 g of the photocatalyst was added into the reaction solution with a volume of 200 mL containing the antibiotics. The reaction suspension was stirred for one hour under the dark condition to achieve a sorption equilibrium. The other experimental details have been reported elsewhere [19].

Fig. 1a displays the XRD patterns of the fabricated photocatalysts. For B-TiO2−x, only the anatase phase of titanium dioxide is observed, which is in well match with the literature [19,27]. The important point is that the intensity of B-TiO2−x peaks is clearly smaller than that of TiO2 [27]. This decrease confirms the generation of OVs and Ti3+ cations in B-TiO2−x [28]. The diffraction pattern for Bi4O5I2 shows peaks for (−4 -11), (402), (−404), (006), and (811) planes, corresponding to its monoclinic phase [29]. After anchoring Bi4O5I2 particles over the surface of B-TiO2, the XRD pattern showed the peaks of B-TiO2−x and Bi4O5I2 counterparts. Furthermore, due to the small amount of anchored CDs, and its effective dispersion, there is not any diffraction peak related to this material.

The FTIR spectra of B-TiO2−x, Bi4O5I2, and B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) samples were provided to characterize their chemical structures, as presented in Fig. 1b. For Bi4O5I2 and B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) samples, functional groups of BiO and BiI bonds were appeared at around 520 cm−1 and 1383 cm−1, respectively [30,31]. For B-TiO2−x and B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) photocatalysts, the absorption band at 720–860 cm− 1 clearly confirmed the vibration of Ti–O–Ti bonds [32]. Furthermore, in all spectra, the peaks located around 1640 cm− 1 and 3200–3600 cm–1 are relevant to the bending and stretching vibrations of water [31,33]. UV–Vis DR spectroscopy was utilized to explore the optical features of the prepared samples. Consistent with the color of the samples, after converting TiO2 to B-TiO2−x and integration with Bi4O5I2 and CDs, the resultant nanocomposites exhibited remarkable visible-light harvesting characteristics (Fig. 1(c, d)). Hence, the ternary B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs nanocomposites could generate many charge carriers under visible light. Besides, using Tauc᾽s plots, the band gap of TiO2 (3.20 eV), B-TiO2−x (1.70 eV), Bi4O5I2 (2.60 eV), B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2-2 (2.42 eV), and B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) (2.40 eV) photocatalysts were obtained, as shown in Fig. 1e.

The morphology, microstructure, and elemental composition of the B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite were studied by FESEM, TEM, HRTEM, and EDX analyses, as shown in Fig. 2. The FESEM, TEM, and HRTEM images affirm the formation of almost sphere-like particles, as observed in Fig. 2(a, b). In the provided HRTEM image, the lattice fringe of 0.31 nm is relevant to the (−4 -11) plane of the orthorhombic phase of Bi4O5I2 [33]. As well as, the lattice fringe of 0.350 nm and 0.320 nm are related to the (101) and (002) planes of tetragonal TiO2−X and CDs, respectively [34,35]. Therefore, the HRTEM image connotes the establishment of heterojunction among B-TiO2−x, Bi4O5I2, and CDs components (Fig. 2c). As manifested in Fig. 2d, O, I, Ti, Bi, and C elements are observed in the EDX spectrum. Moreover, the monotony dispensation of the elements in the ternary photocatalysts is shown in Fig. 2e, which legibly indicates the homogeneous dispensation of the elements into the nanocomposite. Therefore, the EDX analyses in line with the XRD patterns affirm the purity of B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite.

The surface chemical status of B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) photocatalyst was performed through XPS analyses. As observed in Fig. 3a, the general survey spectrum contains Ti 2p, Bi 4 f, O1s, I 3d, and C 1 s peaks, which agrees with the EDX analyses. As manifested in Fig. 3b, the main peaks of Ti 2p were fitted into four peaks at 463.0, 460.2, 458.9, and 457.8 eV, which are assigned to Ti4+ 2p1/2, Ti3+ 2p1/2, Ti4+ 2p3/2, and Ti3+ 2p3/2, respectively. The presence of Ti3+ species is assigned to the generation of oxygen vacancies. Furthermore, the peak at 466.5 eV arises from Bi 4d3/2 [29,36]. In Fig. 3c, the asymmetric peak of O 1 s is deconvoluted to the peaks at 529.4 eV, 530.5 eV, 531.4 eV, and 534.1 eV, which match with the BiO, TiO, oxygen vacancy defect (OV), and oxygen of adsorbed water, respectively [29,37]. The presence of Ti3+ and defect oxygen peaks affirms the presence of B-TiO2−X component in the structure of the ternary photocatalyst. In the C 1 s spectrum, a peak related to nitrous carbon at 289 eV is observed in Fig. 3d, indicating carbon dot particles [38]. In Fig. 3e, two peaks at 159.3 eV and 164.5 eV from the Bi4O5I2 component are allocated to Bi 4 f7/2 and Bi 4 f5/2, respectively [33]. Finally, in Fig. 3f, the peaks at 631.7 eV and 621.4 eV were indexed to the I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2 from the Bi4O5I2 component [39].

Fig. 4(a, b) show the N2 sorption isotherms along with corresponding pore-size distributions of B-TiO2−x, Bi4O5I2, B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2-2, and B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) samples. As evidenced, the isotherms for B-TiO2−x, B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2-2, and B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) samples are relevant to type IV with H2 mesoporous nature, suggesting the presence of mesopores. The specific surface areas obtained for B-TiO2−x, Bi4O5I2, B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2-2, and B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) samples are 144, 6.00, 114, and 129, m2g−1, respectively. Although the surface area of B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) was slightly smaller than B-TiO2−x, the average pore diameter has increased in the ternary photocatalyst, which led to the facilitated reaching of reactant molecules to the active sites. Hence, the noticeable specific surface area of B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2-2 and B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposites relative to Bi4O5I2 reveals that TiO2−X and CDs served as the adsorption sites in the ternary photocatalyst, which could lead to admirable photocatalytic performance ( Table 1).

To study the charges segregation, and transfer in the photocatalysts, PL, EIS, and photocurrent analyses were employed and the outcomes are shown in Fig. 4(c-e). As seen in Fig. 4c, compared with the TiO2, B-TiO2−x, and Bi4O5I2, both of the B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2-2 and B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) samples presented significantly depressed PL intensities, affirming more pronounced carriers segregation (Fig. 4c). Furthermore, the EIS Nyquist plots showed that B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite has the smallest arc radius, and the least Rct (charge transfer resistance) value (Fig. 4d). Briefly, the Rct value for the B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite (Rct = 4.497 kΩ) is almost 11.1, 5.0, 8.0, and 3.4-folds less than those of the TiO2 (Rct = 49.845 kΩ), B-TiO2−x (Rct = 22.672 kΩ), Bi4O5I2 (Rct = 36.096 kΩ), and B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2 (Rct = 15.282 kΩ) photocatalysts, respectively, which implies the least resistance of the ternary nanocomposite for effective transfer of the charges. To confirm the results of EIS and PL analyses, the photocurrent intensity of the samples was provided, and presented in Fig. 4e. As we expected, the ternary TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite has the highest photocurrent intensity [39,40]. Thus, the UV-Vis DRS, PL, EIS, and photocurrent outcomes confirmed that the growth of Bi4O5I2 and CDs particles onto B-TiO2−x photocatalyst considerably enhanced the response to visible light, and improved the segregation efficiency, and transfer of charges, which are from high importance in the photocatalytic processes. The fat-band (Efb), conduction band (ECB), and valence band (EVB) potentials of B-TiO2−x and Bi4O5I2 were calculated by applying the Mott-Schottky plots. About B-TiO2−x, these energies were calculated in the previous work [27]. Fig. 4f shows the Mott-Schottky plot for the Bi4O5I2 sample. As presented, Bi4O5I2 shows a positive slope, which confirms that this material has n-type semiconducting characteristics. The Efb of B-TiO2−x and Bi4O5I2 are specified to be respectively 0.0 and 0.96 V/NHE. Then, the corresponding CB values for B-TiO2−x and Bi4O5I2 were obtained to be respectively − 0.10 and − 1.06 V, and the EVB of B-TiO2−x and Bi4O5I2 were calculated to be respectively + 1.60 and + 1.54 eV, using the equation EVB = ECB + Eg [41].

The photocatalytic performance of TiO2, B-TiO2−x, binary B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2, and ternary B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs photocatalysts was examined for photo-oxidation of TC under visible light. As per results ( Fig. 5a), the photolysis of TC under visible-light illumination is poor, affirming its severe stability. The systems, including TiO2 and B-TiO2−x materials exhibited poor TC removal efficiency of about 26.1% and 35.5%, which can be associated with the rapid recombination of photo-induced charges. After anchoring Bi4O5I2 over the B-TiO2−x, the rate of degradation reaction was promoted, and the B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2-2 nanocomposite showed the best activity among the binary photocatalysts. Interestingly, almost complete removal of TC (99.9%) was achieved by adding carbon dots to the binary B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2-2 photocatalyst. The studies on the photodegradation kinetics of TC revealed that the reactions complied the pseudo-first-order model, and the kinetic constants were calculated, as illustrated in Fig. 5b. As seen, the maximum photodegradation rate was for the B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite (718 ×10 4 min 1), which is almost 49.7, 12.3, and 3.62 folds as high as TiO2, B-TiO2−x, and B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2-2 photocatalysts, respectively. The photocatalytic activities of materials were further examined for degradation of other antibiotics, including CEX and MNZ, and the results are presented in Fig. 5c. Interestingly, the most incredible removal efficiencies of all the studied antibiotics were achieved over the ternary B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite. The photoactivity of the ternary nanocomposite for degradation of CEX and MNZ was 14.4 and 39.8-folds greater than the TiO2, and 4.71 and 11.1 times premier than the B-TiO2−x, respectively. Therefore, the ternary B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite demonstrated outstanding performance for detoxifying various recalcitrant antibiotic wastewaters. The rate constant of TC degradation in the presence of benzoquinone (BQ), ammonium oxalate (AO), and 2-propanol (2-PrOH) as •O2 −, h+, and •OH quenchers reduced from 718 × 10 4 min 1 to 23.0 × 10 4 min 1, 103 × 10 4 min 1, and 673 × 10 4 min 1, respectively. Consequently, it is concluded that •O2 − and h+ have critical role in the photocatalytic degradation of TC antibiotic (Fig. 5d). Furthermore, to affirm the generation of •O2 –, ascorbic acid (another quencher of •O2 –) was also used. The rate constant in the presence of this quencher reduced from 718 × 10 4 min 1 to 70.4 × 10 4 min 1, which signifies the production of •O2 – in the photocatalytic system of B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) (Fig. 5d). Similarly, to confirm that •OH radicals do not play a significant role in the degradation process of TC, terephthalic acid probe method was used. In this method, 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid was produced by the reaction of •OH with terephthalic acid molecules, which can be detected using a PL spectrometer [42,43]. As seen in Fig. 5e, there is not any increase in the intensity of the PL peak with the prolonged illumination time. Therefore, it is concluded that •OH radicals are not the key active species in the degradation of TC.

Based on the outcomes, two plausible charge transfer pathways, including type-II and S-scheme mechanisms were considered, as depicted in Fig. 6. The electron/hole pairs are produced in B-TiO2−X and Bi4O5I2 components upon visible light. If the B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs system involves a type-II carrier transfer mechanism, the electrons tend to migrate automatically from the CB of Bi4O5I2 to that of B-TiO2−X, while holes transfer in the reverse direction. Although this type of charge-transfer mechanism can obviously improve the spatial charge segregation, it weakens the redox potentials of the charges. In the suggested S-scheme system, the photo-induced electrons in the CB of B-TiO2−x transfer via CDs, as mediator, to the VB of Bi4O5I2, leaving more powerful electrons over the CB of Bi4O5I2, and holes in the VB of B-TiO2−X to participate in the production of respectively •O2 – and h+ species, as affirmed by scavenging tests. Therefore, in the S-scheme B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs system, not only the charge carriers are spatially segregated, but also they retain their strong redox potentials, which have key role in the admirable photocatalytic performance [44,45].

To deduce the possible decomposition pathway of TC, the LC-MS analysis was successively carried out. As shown in Fig. 7, TC molecule (m/z = 445) is transformed into various intermediates with low m/z values, including 420, 278, 265, 249, 235, 200, 170, 121, and 106. These results confirm that the molecules of this pollutant gradually turns into small molecules. Furthermore, the biocompatibility of the resultant solution after degradation of TC was studied through growth of wheat seeds. For this purpose, some wheat seeds were cultivated in tap water (control sample), tap water with TC (untreated solution), and the photocatalytic-treated TC solution by B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) photocatalyst. As seen in Fig. 8, the selected seeds does not have significant growth in the untreated solution. In contrast, growth of the seeds in the photocatalytic-treated solution is considerable, and is close to the control sample. Consequently, it is concluded that the treated solution by B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) photocatalyst has considerable capability for cultivation purposes.

The photostability of B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite in the degradation of TC was examined, as shown in Fig. 9a. It is clearly noted that the activity of the ternary photocatalyst considerably retained during four cycles. These results showed that the B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) photocatalyst has significant stability in the degradation reaction of the selected antibiotic. Fig. 9(b and c) depict the crystal structure and morphology of the photocatalyst after four consecutive cycles. As noticed, the morphology and phase structure of the photocatalyst were not changed upon the photocatalysis runs, indicating high stability and reusability of the B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) photocatalyst during the detoxification process.

In a nutshell, novel ternary S-scheme B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs photocatalysts were successfully fabricated via growing Bi4O5I2 and CDs on the surface of B-TiO2−x spheres. The successful integration of B-TiO2−x, Bi4O5I2, and CDs was confirmed by XRD, FTIR, HRTEM, XPS, and EDX characterizing tools. The photocatalytic ability of the synthesized materials was studied against degradations of three antibiotics, including CEX, MNZ, and TC at 25 °C under visible-light exposure supplied by 50 W LED. Amongst the synthesized photocatalysts, the B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs (1 mL) nanocomposite presented remarkable activity, which was 14.4, 39.8, and 49.7 folds as high as TiO2 in degradations of CEX, MNZ, and TC, respectively. The impressively improved photocatalytic activity was attributed to boosted visible-light harvesting capability, significantly segregation of charges, enlarged mean pore diameter, and small resistance for transfer of the charge amongst the counterparts through an S-scheme mechanism. Regarding high stability, impressive performances, and considerable biocompatibility, the novel S-scheme B-TiO2−x/Bi4O5I2/CDs photocatalysts could be promising materials for effectively solving environmental pollution in the future. Scheme 1.

Zahra Salmanzadeh-Jamadi: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Writing – original draft. Aziz Habibi-Yangjeh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Cailing Xu: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Chundong Wang: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to University of Mohaghegh Ardabili for supporting this research. Chundong Wang thanks the funding support from Bintuan Science and Technology Program (Grant No. 2022DB009 and 2020DB002).
